title: Berkshire Hathaway Letters to Shareholders
subtitle: The Annual Letters of Warren Buffett, Compiled as a Stand-Alone Volume. Specially Curated Edition.
author: Warren E. Buffett
editionReferenced: Specially Curated Edition, The Self-Publishing Partnership
themes: buy-and-hold investing as the only rational equity strategy
owner-oriented corporate culture: retain earnings, communicate honestly, align incentives
stock price and business performance diverge regularly: the intelligent investor exploits the gap
analyst expectations are noise: ignore them, report to owners not speculators
intrinsic value vs book value: the divergence is the entire opportunity set
the partnership model: decentralised operations, autonomous owner-managers, no corporate bureaucracy
how to read financial statements: owner earnings as the single most important figure
Berkshire's acquisition history as a case study in patient capital allocation
derivatives folly: hidden leverage, unmeasurable risk, and the danger of financial innovation not understood
economic moats: durable competitive advantage as the defining question in evaluating any business
description: The collected annual shareholder letters of Warren E. Buffett, Chairman of Berkshire Hathaway Inc., spanning 1965 through 2024. First published in various compiled editions since 1997, this specially curated edition issued by The Self-Publishing Partnership (ISBN 9780997316587) assembles the full, unedited letters into a single reference volume. Buffett's letters are the longest-running, most consequential piece of continuous business writing in American corporate history: part annual report, part management manual, part philosophical essay. Each letter teaches the same core ideas — intrinsic value, owner earnings, economic moats, the folly of derivatives, the partnership model — applied to the specific events, acquisitions, mistakes, and market conditions of that year. Together they form a multi-decade case study in how a single-minded owner-orientation compounds into extraordinary results. The letters are freely available at berkshirehathaway.com; this volume makes them searchable, shelf-ready, and sequenced for continuous reading.
toc: 1980–1999: The Moat Era — Building a Conglomerate
2000–2014: Managing Complexity, The Financial Crisis, Derivatives Reckoning
2015–2024: Passing the Baton, The Final Decades
